task: I’ve got a couple of unfamiliar calls and a notice in the mail that both look questionable, and I don’t want to react the wrong way. Can you help me figure out a sensible way to sort out what needs attention now, what can wait, and what I should keep written down?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — sort it in this order:

## 1) Treat both as unverified
- Don’t call back using the number in the call or notice
- Don’t use any link, QR code, or contact info they provided
- Don’t give out codes, passwords, account numbers, or payment info

## 2) Check whether it’s real using your own sources
- Log in to the account yourself through the official site/app
- Use the phone number on your bill, card, or official website
- See whether the same issue shows up there

## 3) Decide what needs attention now
Handle now if it involves:
- money due today
- account access
- shutoff, suspension, or cancellation
- fraud, unauthorized charges, or identity issues

Can wait if it’s:
- a vague request for information
- a notice that doesn’t affect access or payment
- something that doesn’t show up in the real account

## 4) Keep written down
Save:
- the call log
- voicemail
- mailed notice
- envelope, if it has a postmark or return address
- date/time
- number that called
- what was said
- any reference number

## 5) If it’s real, respond in writing when you can
Use email or the company’s message system so there’s a record.

## Simple rule
If the call or notice asks for **money, login access, or personal data**, verify first.  
If it’s **just pressure**, it can usually wait until you confirm it’s real.

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page “verify / act / save” checklist.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But I’d want it tighter — in writing, not just “save the notice.” Keep the envelope too, and note the date it arrived, not just the date on the paper. If there’s a deadline, that goes at the top. 

And I’d separate it a little more:
- **Act now:** anything with a real deadline, shutoff, missed payment, or fraud risk
- **Verify first:** anything asking for money, personal info, or a callback
- **File for later:** vague junk, sales calls, or notices that don’t match any account

I keep a notebook for this kind of thing. Caller name, number, time, what they claimed, and whether I checked it against the real account. That’s the part people skip and then regret it.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — use this order:

## Act now
Anything with:
- a real deadline
- shutoff or suspension risk
- missed payment
- fraud or identity risk

## Verify first
Anything that asks for:
- money
- personal information
- login access
- a callback

## File for later
- vague junk
- sales calls
- notices that don’t match any account

## Keep written down
- caller name
- number
- time
- what they claimed
- whether you checked it against the real account
- the mailed notice
- the envelope
- the date it arrived
- any deadline mentioned

## Simple rule
If it could cost you money, access, or time, verify it using your own source before you respond.

If you want, I can turn this into a short one-page checklist.

user_simulator.text:
That’s better. Tight enough.

I’d keep it exactly like this, maybe even put **deadline** and **shutoff risk** in bold at the top. The envelope matters — postmark, return address, all of it. And yes, the date it arrived, not just what they printed on the paper. People mix that up.

This is the sort of thing that belongs in a notebook, not in your head.
